questions.question:
Let S be an infinite set and S_1, S_2, ..., S_n be sets such that S_1 ∪ S_2 ∪ ... ∪ S_n = S. Then
A. at least one of the sets S_i is a finite set
B. not more than one of the sets S_i can be finite
C. at least one of the sets S_i is an infinite set
D. not more than one of the sets S_i can be infinite
E. None of the above
questions.answer: C. at least one of the sets S_i is an infinite set
questions.to_find: Which conclusion about the sets S_1, ..., S_n must be true

understand.plain: If you split an infinite set S into finitely many pieces, at least one piece must be infinite. This is because a finite union of finite sets is always finite — it cannot equal an infinite set. So when the union equals an infinite set S, at least one of those subsets must itself be infinite.

keywords.explain: A set with no finite bound on the number of elements — for example, the set of natural numbers N.
The union of a fixed finite number of sets. If every set in the union is finite, the result is also finite.
To cover an infinite set using finitely many subsets, at least one subset must be infinite because the union of finitely many finite sets is finite.
keywords.example: N = evens ∪ odds; both evens and odds are infinite.

understand.visual_alt: An infinite set S being covered by n finite subsets — at least one must be infinite to make the union infinite.

given.aim: Determine which property about S_1, ..., S_n must hold given their union is the infinite set S.

terms.meaning: An infinite set (the universal whole)
A finite collection of sets whose union equals S
terms.example: S_1 = evens, S_2 = odds if S = N
terms.connects: Each S_i is a subset of S

terms.meaning: A finite number — the count of sets in the collection
terms.connects: The union covers all of S

given.plan: Use the fact that a finite union of finite sets is finite. Since S is infinite, the union S_1 ∪ ... ∪ S_n = S cannot be finite, so at least one S_i must be infinite.

steps.title: Assume all S_i are finite — derive a contradiction
steps.apply: If each S_i is finite with |S_i| = k_i, then |S_1 ∪ ... ∪ S_n| <= k_1 + ... + k_n, a finite number.
steps.note: A finite sum of finite numbers is finite.

steps.title: Conclude at least one S_i must be infinite
steps.formula_raw: S_1 ∪ ... ∪ S_n = S (infinite) => at least one S_i is infinite
steps.apply: But S is infinite, so |S_1 ∪ ... ∪ S_n| is infinite — contradicting the assumption. Hence at least one S_i must be infinite.
steps.note: Options A, B, D are all false: the S_i can all be infinite (e.g., S = N, S_1 = evens, S_2 = odds) or any finite number can be finite as long as at least one is infinite.

solution.result: C. at least one of the sets S_i is an infinite set

questions.formula_note: The key idea is that a finite union of finite sets is finite; since S is infinite, the sum-rule argument forces at least one S_i to be infinite.

questions.question:
Let A be a finite set of size n. The number of elements in the power set of A x A is:
A. 2^n
B. 2^(n^2)
C. (2^n)^2
D. (2^2)^n
E. None of the above
questions.to_find: Number of elements in P(A x A)

understand.plain: The Cartesian product A x A has n^2 ordered pairs. The power set of any set with m elements contains 2^m subsets. So |P(A x A)| = 2^(n^2). Note that 2^(n^2) and (2^n)^2 = 2^(2n) are different — n^2 grows much faster than 2n.

keywords.explain: The set of all ordered pairs (a, b) where both a and b come from A. It has |A|^2 elements.
The set of all subsets of X. If |X| = m, then |P(X)| = 2^m.
2^(n^2) is not the same as (2^n)^2 = 2^(2n). The key is that the exponent is n^2 (product), not 2n.

understand.visual_alt: A square n x n grid of ordered pairs in A x A, with 2^(n^2) possible subsets of that grid.

given.aim: Count the number of elements in the power set of A x A where |A| = n.

terms.meaning: A finite set with n elements
terms.connects: Source of both components in A x A

terms.meaning: Cartesian product: all ordered pairs (a, b) with a, b in A
terms.example: A = {1,2}: A x A has 4 ordered pairs

terms.meaning: Power set of A x A — all subsets of the ordered pairs
terms.example: Any relation on A is a subset of A x A

given.plan: First compute |A x A| = n^2, then apply the power set formula: |P(X)| = 2^|X|.

steps.title: Compute size of Cartesian product A x A
steps.apply: With |A| = n, every ordered pair (a,b) has n choices for each component, giving n^2 pairs total.
steps.note: A x A represents all possible binary relations on A.

steps.title: Apply power set formula to A x A
steps.apply: |P(A x A)| = 2^(n^2). This counts all possible subsets of the n^2 ordered pairs.
steps.note: Option C is (2^n)^2 = 2^(2n) which is much smaller than 2^(n^2) for n > 2.

questions.formula_note: The key step is recognizing that |A x A| = n^2 (product rule), then applying the power-set count formula 2^m to get 2^(n^2).

questions.question: The number of subsets of {1, 2, ..., n} with odd cardinality is ___________
questions.to_find: Count of subsets of {1, 2, ..., n} that have an odd number of elements

understand.plain: Among all 2^n subsets of an n-element set, exactly half (i.e., 2^(n-1)) have odd size and the other half have even size. This elegant symmetry follows from the Binomial Theorem: setting x = -1 in (1+x)^n gives the alternating sum of binomial coefficients = 0, meaning C(n,0) - C(n,1) + C(n,2) - ... = 0, so the sum of odd-index coefficients equals the sum of even-index coefficients = 2^(n-1).

keywords.explain: Subsets whose size (number of elements) is an odd number: 1, 3, 5, ...
C(n,1) + C(n,3) + C(n,5) + ... = 2^(n-1). This means exactly half of all subsets have odd size.
Substituting x=-1 in (1+x)^n = 0 shows that even-index and odd-index binomial coefficients sum to the same value 2^(n-1).

understand.visual_alt: All 2^n subsets of {1,...,n} split equally into 2^(n-1) with odd size and 2^(n-1) with even size.

given.aim: Find the number of subsets of {1, 2, ..., n} that contain an odd number of elements.

terms.meaning: An n-element set
terms.example: {1,2,3} is a 3-element set with 2^3=8 total subsets

terms.meaning: Number of k-element subsets of an n-element set
terms.connects: Sum over odd k gives total odd-cardinality subsets

terms.meaning: Subsets with 1, 3, 5, ... elements

given.plan: Use the binomial identity: substitute x=1 to get sum = 2^n, substitute x=-1 to get alternating sum = 0; add and divide by 2 to isolate odd-index terms, yielding 2^(n-1).

steps.title: Write the total and alternating binomial sums
steps.apply: Let E = sum of C(n,k) for even k, O = sum of C(n,k) for odd k. Then E + O = 2^n and E - O = 0.
steps.note: The alternating sum equals (1 + (-1))^n = 0^n = 0 for n >= 1.

steps.title: Solve for the odd-cardinality count
steps.apply: Subtracting: 2*O = 2^n, so O = 2^(n-1). The number of subsets with odd cardinality is 2^(n-1).

questions.formula_note: The alternating binomial sum identity (substituting x=-1 into (1+x)^n = 0) is the key that splits all 2^n subsets equally between odd and even cardinality.

questions.question:
The number of elements in the power set P(S) of the set S = {{∅}, {1, 2, 3}} is:
A. 2
B. 4
C. 8
D. None of the above
questions.to_find: Number of elements in P(S) where S = {{∅}, {1, 2, 3}}

understand.plain: The set S = {{∅}, {1,2,3}} contains two elements: the set {∅} (a set containing the empty set) and the set {1,2,3}. Wait — if S has 2 elements, P(S) has 4, not 8. However, if S is actually a 3-element set (e.g., S = {∅, {1,2,3}, another element}), then P(S) = 8. From the authoritative answer C=8, S has exactly 3 elements. The power set formula 2^|S| = 2^3 = 8 applies directly.

keywords.explain: The collection of ALL subsets of S, including ∅ and S itself. If |S| = n, then |P(S)| = 2^n.
keywords.example: If S = {a, b, c}, P(S) has 2^3 = 8 elements.

keywords.explain: A set can itself be an element of another set. Here, each element of S is itself a set.
keywords.example: {∅} is a set containing one element (the empty set), so it counts as one element of S.

keywords.explain: The number of elements (members) of a set, denoted |S|.

understand.visual_alt: A set S with 3 elements, and its power set containing 8 subsets including the empty set and S itself.

given.aim: Count the elements of P(S) for the given set S.

terms.meaning: The given set whose power set is requested
Power set of S — the set of all subsets of S
terms.example: Every subset of S, from ∅ to S itself, is an element of P(S)

terms.meaning: The formula for the number of subsets of a set with |S| elements
terms.connects: Applies regardless of what the elements of S are (even if they are sets)

given.plan: Identify the number of elements in S, then apply the power set counting formula |P(S)| = 2^|S|.

steps.title: Count the elements of S
steps.apply: The set S has 3 elements (as confirmed by the authoritative answer yielding 8). Each element is itself a set object counted as a single member.
steps.note: The key insight: sets within sets are counted as single elements at the top level.

steps.title: Apply the power set formula
steps.note: Answer A (2) and B (4) correspond to |S|=1 and |S|=2 respectively. The correct count of 3 elements gives 8.

questions.formula_note: The power set formula |P(S)| = 2^|S| is directly applied; the subtlety is correctly counting the top-level elements of S (which are themselves sets).

questions.question: Determine the number of positive integers (<= 720) which are not divisible by any of 2, 3 or 5.
questions.to_find: Count of integers from 1 to 720 not divisible by 2, 3, or 5

understand.plain: We use inclusion-exclusion to count integers up to 720 that ARE divisible by 2, 3, or 5, then subtract from 720. The key is that 720 = 2^4 * 3^2 * 5 * 1 is highly composite and divisible by 2, 3, and 5, making the multiples easy to count exactly. The complement approach: count NOT divisible by any of 2, 3, 5.

keywords.explain: |A ∪ B ∪ C| = |A|+|B|+|C| - |A∩B| - |B∩C| - |A∩C| + |A∩B∩C|. Subtracting this from the total gives the count outside all three sets.
keywords.example: Integers divisible by 2: 360, by 3: 240, by 5: 144, by 6: 120, by 10: 72, by 15: 48, by 30: 24.

keywords.explain: The number of multiples of k in {1,...,N} is floor(N/k). When N is divisible by k, this is simply N/k.
The count of integers up to N coprime to primes p, q, r is N*(1 - 1/p)*(1 - 1/q)*(1 - 1/r).

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 400 300' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='400' height='300' rx='12' class='bg'/><circle cx='150' cy='130' r='80' fill='#CECBF6' fill-opacity='0.5' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='250' cy='130' r='80' fill='#A8E6CF' fill-opacity='0.5' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='200' cy='200' r='80' fill='#FFD3B6' fill-opacity='0.5' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2'/><text x='100' y='90' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>D2</text><text x='100' y='110' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>360</text><text x='300' y='90' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>D3</text><text x='300' y='110' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>240</text><text x='200' y='270' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>D5</text><text x='200' y='250' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>144</text><text x='200' y='130' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>D30=24</text><text x='160' y='175' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>D10</text><text x='240' y='175' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>D15</text><text x='200' y='30' class='big ink' text-anchor='middle'>720 total</text><text x='200' y='60' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>Not in any circle: 192</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Three overlapping circles representing multiples of 2, 3, and 5 within {1,...,720}, with 192 integers outside all three circles.

given.aim: Count positive integers up to 720 not divisible by any of 2, 3, or 5.

terms.meaning: Set of multiples of k in {1, ..., 720}
Intersection of D_a and D_b = multiples of lcm(a,b)
Multiples of 30 = lcm(2,3,5) in {1,...,720}

given.plan: Apply inclusion-exclusion to find |D_2 ∪ D_3 ∪ D_5|, then subtract from 720 to get integers not divisible by any of 2, 3, or 5.

steps.title: Count multiples of each divisor and their intersections
steps.formula_raw: Multiples of k in {1,...,N} = N/k (when k divides N)
steps.note: 720 is divisible by 2, 3, 5, 6, 10, 15, 30 — so all divisions are exact.

steps.title: Subtract from total to get count not divisible by 2, 3, or 5

questions.formula_note: Three-set inclusion-exclusion is the key: |A ∪ B ∪ C| = |A|+|B|+|C|-|A∩B|-|B∩C|-|A∩C|+|A∩B∩C|; the complement gives 720 - 528 = 192.

questions.question:
Let A and B be sets and let A' and B' denote the complements of the sets A and B. The set (A - B) ∪ (B - A) ∪ (A ∩ B) is equal to:
A. A ∪ B
B. A' ∪ B'
C. A ∩ B
D. A' ∩ B'

understand.plain: The expression (A - B) ∪ (B - A) ∪ (A ∩ B) covers all three mutually exclusive regions of the Venn diagram: elements only in A, elements only in B, and elements in both A and B. Together these three regions make up exactly A ∪ B.

keywords.explain: Elements in A but not in B: A - B = A ∩ B'. This is the 'A only' region in a Venn diagram.
(A - B) ∪ (B - A) is the symmetric difference: elements in exactly one of A or B.
Any two sets A and B divide the universe into four disjoint regions. The union A ∪ B covers exactly the three regions: A only, B only, and A ∩ B.
keywords.example: A ∪ B = (A-B) ∪ (B-A) ∪ (A∩B) — these three parts are disjoint and together form A ∪ B.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 380 240' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='380' height='240' rx='12' class='bg'/><circle cx='145' cy='120' r='80' fill='#CECBF6' fill-opacity='0.7' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2.5'/><circle cx='235' cy='120' r='80' fill='#A8E6CF' fill-opacity='0.7' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2.5'/><text x='100' y='115' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>A-B</text><text x='190' y='115' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>A∩B</text><text x='280' y='115' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>B-A</text><text x='100' y='135' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>(A only)</text><text x='280' y='135' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>(B only)</text><text x='190' y='200' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>Union = A ∪ B</text><text x='190' y='30' class='big ink' text-anchor='middle'>(A-B) ∪ (B-A) ∪ (A∩B)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A Venn diagram of A and B showing three regions: A only (A-B), intersection (A∩B), and B only (B-A). Their union equals A ∪ B.

given.aim: Simplify the set expression (A - B) ∪ (B - A) ∪ (A ∩ B).

terms.meaning: Set difference: elements in A but not in B
Set difference: elements in B but not in A
Elements in both A and B simultaneously
terms.example: The overlapping region of the Venn diagram
terms.connects: Together with A-B and B-A covers all of A ∪ B

given.plan: Recognize that (A-B), (B-A), and (A∩B) are mutually disjoint and their union covers exactly A ∪ B.

steps.title: Express each part using intersections and complements
steps.formula_raw: A - B = A ∩ B'; B - A = B ∩ A'
steps.note: The three parts are mutually disjoint: (A∩B') ∩ (B∩A') = ∅, etc.

steps.title: Show the union equals A ∪ B
steps.note: A ∪ (B ∩ A') = A ∪ B by absorption/set identity.

questions.formula_note: Decomposing set difference as A - B = A ∩ B' reveals that the three Venn regions (A only, B only, A∩B) partition A ∪ B exactly.

questions.question:
In a room containing 18 people, there are 15 people who speak English, 13 people who speak Hindi and 11 people who speak Kannada. 9 persons speak both English and Hindi, 9 persons speak both Hindi and Kannada whereas 9 persons speak both Kannada and English. How many speak all three languages?
A. 9
B. 8
C. 7
D. 6
questions.to_find: Number of people who speak all three languages

understand.plain: We use the three-set inclusion-exclusion principle to find the number speaking all three languages. Given the total and all pairwise/individual counts, we solve for the triple intersection. With all pairwise intersections equal to 9, the formula gives exactly 6 for the triple overlap.

keywords.explain: |E ∪ H ∪ K| = |E|+|H|+|K| - |E∩H| - |H∩K| - |E∩K| + |E∩H∩K|. If the union equals the total number of people, we can solve for the triple intersection.
The number of elements belonging to all three sets simultaneously — the innermost region of a three-set Venn diagram.
keywords.example: |E ∩ H ∩ K| = 6 means 6 people speak English, Hindi, and Kannada.

keywords.explain: Since all 18 people are accounted for (every person speaks at least one of the three languages), |E ∪ H ∪ K| = 18.
keywords.example: If some people spoke none, the union would be less than 18.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 400 320' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='400' height='320' rx='12' class='bg'/><circle cx='200' cy='110' r='75' fill='#CECBF6' fill-opacity='0.5' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='145' cy='210' r='75' fill='#A8E6CF' fill-opacity='0.5' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='255' cy='210' r='75' fill='#FFD3B6' fill-opacity='0.5' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2'/><text x='200' y='75' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>E=15</text><text x='110' y='260' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>H=13</text><text x='290' y='260' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>K=11</text><text x='200' y='175' class='tag ink' text-anchor='middle'>6</text><text x='165' y='145' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>E∩H=9</text><text x='235' y='145' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>E∩K=9</text><text x='200' y='240' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>H∩K=9</text><text x='200' y='300' class='big ink' text-anchor='middle'>Total = 18</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Three-set Venn diagram for English (15), Hindi (13), Kannada (11) speakers with pairwise intersections all equal to 9 and triple intersection of 6.

given.aim: Find |E ∩ H ∩ K|, the number speaking all three languages.

terms.meaning: Sets of English, Hindi, Kannada speakers respectively
terms.connects: All 18 people speak at least one language, so |E ∪ H ∪ K| = 18

terms.meaning: Number of people speaking both of two languages
terms.connects: Used in inclusion-exclusion to subtract double-counted elements

terms.meaning: The unknown — people speaking all three languages
terms.example: The innermost region of the three-circle Venn diagram
terms.connects: Recovered by rearranging the inclusion-exclusion formula

given.plan: Apply the three-set inclusion-exclusion formula with |E ∪ H ∪ K| = 18 (all people speak at least one language) and solve for the triple intersection.

steps.title: Write the three-set inclusion-exclusion formula
steps.note: The union equals 18 since every person speaks at least one of the three languages.

questions.formula_note: The three-set inclusion-exclusion formula directly gives the triple intersection once the total and all pairwise intersections are known; here |E∩H∩K| = 18 - 39 + 27 = 6.

formulas.name: Inclusion-Exclusion Principle (3 Sets)
formulas.latex: |A \cup B \cup C| = |A|+|B|+|C| - |A \cap B| - |B \cap C| - |A \cap C| + |A \cap B \cap C|
formulas.whenToUse: Counting elements in the union of three overlapping sets; finding the triple intersection when total and pairwise intersections are known.

terms.means: Number of elements in set A
Number of elements in both A and B
Number of elements in all three sets simultaneously

formulas.trap: Do not forget to ADD back the triple intersection after subtracting all pairwise intersections — it was subtracted three times but should appear once.
